title: Prepositions of time: in / on / at

explain.rule: English uses three main prepositions of time: AT, ON, and IN. AT is for specific clock times and fixed expressions (at 7pm, at midnight, at Christmas). ON is for days and dates (on Monday, on 3rd March, on my birthday). IN is for longer periods — months, seasons, years, centuries, and parts of the day (in July, in summer, in 2024, in the morning). A useful way to remember: AT is the smallest/most precise, ON is medium, IN is the largest.
explain.why_matters: Time prepositions appear in almost every sentence about when something happens. Getting them right is the difference between sounding natural and sounding like a learner. English does not always match Hebrew — in Hebrew you would use ב for many of these situations, but in English the choice between at/on/in changes the meaning.
explain.hebrew_note: In Hebrew, the preposition ב (be) is used for most time expressions — בשעה שבע, ביום שני, בינואר, בקיץ. In English you must choose between at, on, and in depending on the type of time expression. This is a common source of errors for Hebrew speakers who tend to use 'in' for everything or mix up 'in' and 'on' for days.
explain.tip: Think of a funnel getting smaller: IN (big periods — months, years, seasons) → ON (specific days and dates) → AT (exact times and moments). The bigger the time unit, the bigger the preposition. Also: no preposition with 'last', 'next', 'this', 'every', 'yesterday', 'tomorrow' — these stand alone.

form.AT: at + clock time: at 7pm, at noon, at midnight, at half past three | at + fixed expressions: at Christmas, at Easter, at the weekend (British English)
form.ON: on + day: on Monday, on Fridays | on + date: on 5th April, on 14 March 2020 | on + special day: on my birthday, on New Year's Day
form.IN: in + month: in January | in + season: in summer, in winter | in + year: in 2024, in 1995 | in + century: in the 21st century | in + part of day: in the morning, in the afternoon, in the evening (NOT at night — exception)

exceptions.note: NOT 'in the night' — we say 'at night' as a fixed expression

exceptions.expression: at the weekend (British) / on the weekend (American)
exceptions.note: Both are correct — depends on dialect
Specific points in time → at, not in
last Monday, next Friday, this morning, every summer — no preposition

exceptions.expression: in the morning BUT on Monday morning
exceptions.note: Part of day alone → in. Part of day + specific day → on

good_examples.sentence: The meeting starts at nine o'clock.
good_examples.audio_text: The meeting starts at nine o'clock.

good_examples.sentence: I was born on a Tuesday in March.
good_examples.audio_text: I was born on a Tuesday in March.

good_examples.sentence: She usually goes running in the morning.
good_examples.audio_text: She usually goes running in the morning.

good_examples.sentence: The shop is closed on Sundays.
good_examples.audio_text: The shop is closed on Sundays.

good_examples.sentence: It gets very cold here in winter.
good_examples.audio_text: It gets very cold here in winter.

good_examples.sentence: I always feel tired at night.
good_examples.audio_text: I always feel tired at night.
good_examples.note: at night is a fixed expression — exception to the 'in' pattern
lunchtime = specific point in day → at

bad_examples.reason: Days use ON, not in. In Hebrew ביום שני uses ב, but in English days always take ON.
Clock times use AT. 7am is a specific moment → at 7am.
Years use IN. 1995 is a year (large period) → in 1995.
Night is a fixed expression with AT — 'at night', not 'in the night'.
Parts of the day (morning, afternoon, evening) use IN when used alone.

exercises.question: The train arrives ___ 6:30 in the evening.
exercises.explanation: Clock times use AT: at 6:30, at noon, at midnight.
exercises.hebrew_note: שעות מדויקות = at בעברית היינו אומרים ב-6:30

exercises.question: She was born ___ October.
exercises.explanation: Months use IN: in October, in January, in March.

exercises.question: We have a meeting ___ Monday morning.
exercises.hint: Day + part of day → which preposition? ON Monday morning (not in, because a specific day is named)
exercises.explanation: When a specific day is mentioned, use ON — even with a part of the day: on Monday morning.

exercises.question: He moved to London ___ 2018.
exercises.explanation: Years use IN: in 2018, in 1995, in 2024.

exercises.question: I always read ___ the evening.
exercises.hint: Part of the day (alone, no specific day) → which preposition?
exercises.explanation: Parts of the day alone use IN: in the morning, in the afternoon, in the evening.

exercises.question: The party is ___ Saturday night.
exercises.hint: Specific day → which preposition? Even with 'night' here.
exercises.explanation: Saturday makes this specific → ON Saturday night. But: I can't sleep at night (no specific day).

exercises.question: It's very cold here ___ winter.
exercises.explanation: Seasons use IN: in winter, in summer, in spring, in autumn.

exercises.question: The concert starts ___ eight o'clock.
exercises.explanation: Clock times always use AT.

exercises.question: She called me ___ midnight.
exercises.explanation: midnight and noon are specific points in time → at midnight, at noon.

exercises.question: The company was founded ___ the 1980s.
exercises.hint: Decade → in (like a year, but a range)
exercises.explanation: Decades use IN: in the 1980s, in the 1990s, in the 2000s.

exercises.question: I have a dentist appointment ___ 14th March.
exercises.explanation: Specific dates use ON: on 14th March, on 5th April.

exercises.question: We always visit family ___ Christmas.
exercises.explanation: Festival periods use AT: at Christmas, at Easter, at Passover, at New Year.

exercises.question: He was born ___ a cold winter morning in 1991.
exercises.explanation: 'A cold winter morning' functions like a specific day → on.

exercises.question: I can't sleep ___ night — I have too many things on my mind.
exercises.hint: night alone (no specific day) = fixed expression → at
exercises.explanation: 'At night' is a fixed expression. Use it whenever you mean 'during the night hours' in general.

exercises.question: The office closes ___ 5:30 every day.
exercises.explanation: Clock times use AT: at 5:30.

exercises.question: I was born ___ a Thursday.
exercises.explanation: Days of the week use ON: on a Thursday, on Mondays.

exercises.question: She finished university ___ June 2022.
exercises.explanation: Month + year: in June 2022. The IN comes before the month.

exercises.question: The best time to visit is ___ spring.
exercises.explanation: Seasons use IN: in spring, in summer, in autumn, in winter.

exercises.question: I'll meet you ___ the weekend.
exercises.hint: British English: at the weekend. American: on the weekend.
exercises.explanation: In British English: at the weekend. In American English: on the weekend. Both are accepted.

exercises.question: Which is correct?
exercises.answer: I last saw him on 3rd April.
exercises.explanation: Specific dates use ON: on 3rd April.
distractors: I last saw him in 3rd April.
I last saw him at 3rd April.
I last saw him in the 3rd April.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'The meeting is in Monday.'
exercises.explanation: Days of the week use ON. In Hebrew ביום שני but in English: on Monday.
exercises.hebrew_note: ימים בשבוע = ON. ביום שני = on Monday

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'I wake up in 6am every day.'
exercises.answer: I wake up at 6am every day.
exercises.explanation: Clock times use AT: at 6am.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'She was born on 1998.'
exercises.explanation: Years use IN: in 1998.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'I love walking in the night.'
exercises.hint: Night alone (no specific day) → fixed expression
exercises.explanation: 'At night' is a fixed expression. 'In the night' is not standard.

exercises.question: Find and fix the mistake: 'He arrived on the morning.'
exercises.hint: Part of the day alone → which preposition?
exercises.explanation: Parts of the day alone: in the morning, in the afternoon, in the evening.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: The bus leaves at seven o'clock.
exercises.explanation: Clock times use AT: at seven o'clock.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: She was born in spring in 1990.
exercises.explanation: Both season and year use IN. She was born in spring in 1990.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: I'll see you on Friday morning.
exercises.hint: Specific day + morning → on (day is named)
exercises.explanation: When a specific day is mentioned, use ON even with a part of the day.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: The shop is closed on Saturdays.
exercises.hint: Repeated day of the week → on
exercises.explanation: Days of the week use ON whether singular or plural.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.explanation: 'At night' is a fixed expression: she doesn't work at night.

speak.sentence: The meeting starts at ten o'clock on Monday morning.
chunks: The meeting starts at ten o'clock
speak.phonetic_hints: 'O'clock' = uh-KLOK — stress on second syllable. 'Monday' = MUN-day.

speak.sentence: I was born in July in nineteen ninety-two.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'July' = joo-LY — stress on second syllable. 'Ninety' = NYE-tee — stress on first syllable.

speak.sentence: She usually goes to the gym in the evening after work.
chunks: She usually goes to the gym

speak.sentence: The concert is on Saturday the twenty-third of March.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Concert' = KON-sert — stress on first syllable. 'Twenty-third' = TWEN-tee-THURD.

speak.sentence: It's freezing cold here in the winter, especially in January and February.
chunks: It's freezing cold here in the winter,
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Freezing' = FREE-zing. 'January' = JAN-yoo-air-ee — four syllables. 'February' = FEB-roo-air-ee — say it carefully.

speak.sentence: I always feel more relaxed at the weekend.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Relaxed' = rih-LAKST. 'Weekend' = WEEK-end — stress on first syllable.

speak.sentence: The shop closes at nine o'clock at night.
chunks: The shop closes at nine o'clock
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Closes' = KLOH-ziz. 'O'clock' = uh-KLOK. Remember: at night (not in the night).

speak.sentence: She moved to a new city in the spring of two thousand and twenty.
chunks: She moved to a new city
in the spring of two thousand and twenty.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Moved' = MOOVD. 'Spring' = SPRING. 'Thousand' = THOW-zund — the 'th' sound, not 't'.
